---
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

<!-- JHSB outline: ~5 paragraphs, no subsection headers. Continuous prose.
P1: Historical/theoretical anchor + core finding
P2: Findings summary + mechanism + intervention point
P3: Theoretical contribution + practical implications + limitations (integrated)
P4: Future directions (each tied to a specific limitation or finding)
P5: Closing (zoom out, normative, forward-looking) -->

A long tradition in sociology treats social identities as structuring forces that shape behavior through mechanisms of group conformity, boundary maintenance, and durable dispositions [@tajfel_integrative_1979; @bourdieu_distinction_1984; @lamont_dignity_2000].
In the health domain specifically, @cockerham_health_2005 argued that health behaviors are not reducible to individual agency but are structured by collective, group-level forces — an insight that health lifestyles research has since confirmed across multiple populations and behavioral domains [@mollborn_contributions_2021].
Our findings extend this tradition by establishing partisan identity as a strong, independent predictor of health behavior.
Partisan differences in social contacts, mask-wearing, and vaccine acceptance during the COVID-19 pandemic were substantial — with the partisan gap in mask-wearing exceeding differences by race, gender, or education — and proved remarkably resistant to every alternative explanation we tested, consistent with @clinton_partisan_2021's finding that partisanship was roughly 27 times more important than public health concerns in predicting pandemic mobility behavior.
They persisted within shared policy environments, across geographic regions, regardless of local partisan composition, and after controlling for demographics.
This pattern of "portable polarization" — behavioral differences that prove remarkably robust across diverse contexts, even as partisans adjust modestly toward local norms — is consistent with the claim that partisanship has become a fundamental social identity in American life, one that shapes health behavior directly rather than merely correlating with the environments in which partisans are embedded.

The specific patterns of variation in partisan gaps offer insight into the mechanisms through which identity operates.
Social contacts — a private, high-stakes behavior — provide a particularly informative theoretical test because staying home, avoiding gatherings, and reducing daily interactions are largely unobservable by one's political community; they cannot easily function as signals of partisan allegiance in the way that visible behaviors can.
Contact gaps narrowed during high-mortality periods, suggesting that concrete, undeniable consequences can temporarily override partisan divergence when the behavior is private and the stakes are immediate.
Mask-wearing — a visible, low-cost behavior — showed steadily widening polarization regardless of severity, consistent with its emergence as what @goffman_stigma_1963 would recognize as a stigma symbol: a publicly legible marker that instantly communicates group membership, making it a site of impression management rather than a calculated response to risk.
Research on social norms and COVID-19 compliance has distinguished public-facing from private protective behaviors, finding that mandatory mask policies shaped not only compliance but social perceptions of others' behavior [@betsch_social_2020], while work on identity-protective cognition has shown that culturally diverse individuals selectively credit and dismiss evidence in patterns that reflect their group's beliefs — a dynamic that intensifies rather than diminishes among the most scientifically literate [@kahan_misconceptions_2017].
@baxter-king_how_2022 gesture toward this visibility mechanism, noting that mask-wearing is "publicly observable" compared to other mitigation strategies and finding that local partisan context shaped Republican but not Democratic mask-wearing.
Our findings extend this framework by systematically comparing behaviors that differ in social visibility within the same sample — a test that prior work, including Baxter-King et al., was not designed to make.
That masking diverged more sharply by partisanship than contact reduction did demonstrates that the symbolic dimension of health behavior was politically activated in a way that private protective behaviors were not.
The logic of this comparison is diagnostic: if partisan gaps were driven entirely by generalized health lifestyle dispositions or divergent risk perceptions, both behaviors should show similar partisan divergence, since both reduce disease transmission.
An alternative reading might attribute the divergence to differences in personal cost rather than social visibility, since reducing contacts entails greater material sacrifice than wearing a mask.
But this account generates the wrong temporal prediction: if behavioral gaps primarily reflect cost sensitivity, mask gaps should also narrow during mortality surges, since the health cost of not masking rises alongside mortality.
Instead, only the private, high-cost behavior responded to heightened risk while the visible, low-cost behavior did not, a pattern more consistent with a social observability mechanism in which the identity costs of deviating from one's group norm on a publicly legible behavior offset the health benefits of compliance.
Had masking and contact patterns tracked each other closely within partisan groups, that would suggest a coherent underlying behavioral disposition, the kind of integrated health lifestyle that @cockerham_health_2005 describes.
That they diverged, specifically in the direction predicted by social visibility, points instead to identity-driven processes operating selectively on publicly observable behaviors.
We note, however, that the widening mask gap is consistent with more than one identity-based mechanism.
The pattern could reflect active signaling, in which partisans consciously display or withhold masks to communicate group membership, but it could equally reflect the gradual crystallization of group norms: Democrats habituating to mask-wearing as an unremarkable default within their social networks while Republicans consolidated an anti-mask norm within theirs.
These mechanisms are not mutually exclusive, and both are fundamentally identity-driven, but they differ in the degree of intentionality involved.
Our data cannot distinguish between them, though the steady, monotonic widening of the gap over time is perhaps more consistent with norm crystallization than with episodic signaling in response to specific encounters.
What our data do establish is that whatever the specific mechanism, it operated selectively on the visible behavior and not on the private one, confirming that social observability is a necessary condition for the identity process at work.

The geographic distribution of partisan gaps further illuminates how context and identity interact.
Where gaps were smaller, as in the South, the narrowing reflected Democrats behaving less cautiously rather than Republicans becoming more cautious, a pattern visible across demographic and contextual subgroups (@sec-behavior-levels-table): context shifted absolute levels of behavior without closing the partisan divide.
This asymmetric pattern is consistent with @baxter-king_how_2022's finding that local partisan context shaped Republican but not Democratic mask-wearing, and suggests more broadly that the two parties' health behaviors may be anchored to different reference points.
If Republican behavior is primarily shaped by nationally circulated partisan cues and media ecosystems that do not vary much by region, while Democratic behavior responds more to local norms and conditions, we would expect exactly the pattern we observe: gaps that persist in every region but vary in magnitude as local conditions pull one group toward the behavioral baseline of its surroundings.
Regional variation in gap size therefore does not undermine the portable polarization thesis; rather, it specifies the channel through which context operates, shifting the behavior of one group more than the other while leaving the fundamental partisan divide intact.

Earlier work using GPS mobility data found that partisan identity moderated responses to elite messaging from the pandemic's earliest days: Democratic-leaning counties responded about twice as strongly to governors' stay-at-home recommendations as Republican-leaning counties, even after accounting for demographics, occupational structure, and local case rates [@grossman_political_2020].
Our individual-level data are consistent with this early divergence but reveal an additional dynamic: the behavioral gaps we measure *widened* over time, particularly for visible behaviors like mask-wearing, suggesting that what began as differential responsiveness to elite cues crystallized into durable identity markers as the pandemic progressed.
The temporal pattern for mask-wearing mirrors what @clinton_partisan_2021 documented for stated concern: in their data, Democrats maintained consistently high concern while Republicans became increasingly less concerned over time, with the gap accelerating after partisan elite cues intensified.
Our mask-wearing data follow this same widening trajectory — but our contact data do not, revealing a critical disconnect between stated concern and actual behavior.
During the winter 2020-21 surge, Republicans reported their lowest concern levels yet reduced contacts substantially, suggesting that expressed attitudes had become performative signals of group allegiance while behavior tracked objective conditions.
This finding challenges the assumption, common in survey-based health research, that stated attitudes reliably predict or explain protective behavior on politicized health topics.
More fundamentally, the persistence of partisan behavioral gaps even after accounting for perceived severity suggests that something beyond risk cognition is driving behavior — the behavior has decoupled from the belief.
This residual gap is precisely where identity-based theories do their explanatory work: social categorization processes that make partisan membership salient in everyday encounters [@tajfel_integrative_1979], impression management pressures that make visible behaviors like mask-wearing sites of stigma or prestige within one's community [@goffman_stigma_1963], and symbolic boundary maintenance that transforms health practices into markers of group belonging [@lamont_dignity_2000].
The practical implication is that public health messaging designed to increase reported concern may change what people say without changing what they do — and that interventions targeting the objective risk environment (e.g., making consequences visible and local) may be more effective than those targeting stated attitudes.
Experimental evidence underscores the identity dynamics at work: [@pink_elite_2021] found that Republican elite endorsements increased vaccination intentions among Republicans, while Democratic endorsements produced backlash, actually reducing Republicans' willingness to encourage others to vaccinate.
This suggests that effective public health communication on politicized topics may need to work *through* partisan identity rather than against it — though such an approach risks further entrenching the very polarization it seeks to navigate.

This study contributes to the sociology of health by demonstrating that partisan identity operates as an independent driver of health behavior, not reducible to policy, information, or place — extending recent work in this journal showing that partisan polarization structures trust in scientific authorities in ways that intensify among the most knowledgeable [@gauchat_partisan_2025] from the domain of attitudes to the domain of behavior.
The competing-theories framework offers a generalizable approach for descriptive studies seeking to constrain causal explanations: by systematically testing where each theory predicts gaps should narrow, we identify the explanation that survives the most stringent comparisons.
Several limitations warrant consideration alongside these contributions.
Our data are observational and cross-sectional within each wave, precluding strong causal claims; we observe associations between partisan identity and health behavior but cannot definitively rule out unmeasured confounders.
All behavioral measures are self-reported, raising several interrelated concerns about partisan survey bias.
Democrats may under-report contacts and over-report mask usage to conform to perceived group expectations, just as Republicans may do the reverse; @yair_how_2020 have shown that standard survey items can confound sincere partisan differences with expressive responding — insincere answers that communicate partisan allegiance rather than actual perceptions or behaviors.
If such dynamics operate in our data, we would be capturing identity signaling in the survey response itself rather than in the underlying behavior — though notably, this would constitute the same identity-driven mechanism we theorize about, operating at the level of survey response rather than health behavior.
Additionally, @clinton_reluctant_2022 documented differential partisan nonresponse in surveys conducted during this period, with Republicans — particularly those least engaged with public health guidance — being less likely to participate.
If this selection operates in our data, it would likely *attenuate* the partisan gaps we estimate, because the Republicans missing from our sample are plausibly those with the most contacts and least mask usage; our estimates would thus represent a conservative lower bound on the true behavioral divergence (see @sec-weight-sensitivity for further discussion).
The specificity of our measures — actual contact counts for the previous day, mask usage during specific recalled interactions — reduces the scope for expressive responding relative to abstract attitudinal items, but cannot eliminate it.
Crucially, however, even if expressive responding inflates the mask-wearing gap, the contact measure provides a partial check: counting how many people you spoke with face-to-face yesterday is a concrete, verifiable recall task with less room for partisan motivated reasoning than reporting whether you "usually" wear a mask, and partisan gaps in contacts persist even during high-mortality periods when expressive incentives to minimize concern were strongest among Republicans.
Our survey waves span April 2020 to May 2021, capturing a critical period but not the later Delta and Omicron waves or the longer arc of vaccine polarization; @wallace_excess_2023 documented that the partisan mortality gap widened substantially after vaccines became available, suggesting that the behavioral divergence we observe likely intensified beyond our observation window.
These temporal boundaries are also a strength: by examining a period when partisan scripts were actively forming, we observe polarization as it developed rather than after it crystallized.

Each limitation suggests a direction for future work.
Longitudinal designs following the same individuals over time could establish whether partisan identity causes behavioral change or whether partisan self-sorting into different behavioral patterns precedes identity consolidation.
Comparative studies across health crises — including non-pandemic contexts such as gun violence, environmental health, or reproductive health — could test whether portable polarization is specific to COVID-19 or characterizes partisan identity's influence on health behavior more broadly; evidence that ideology shaped vaccine hesitancy even before the pandemic [@baumgaertner_influence_2018] suggests that the dynamics we document may have deeper roots.
Experimental work could examine whether making mortality consequences salient narrows partisan gaps in real time, providing a causal test of the severity mechanism we observe descriptively.
Finally, network-based studies could illuminate the social pathways through which partisan behavioral norms are transmitted and reinforced — @mosleh_shared_2021 found that shared partisanship dramatically increases social tie formation, suggesting that partisan homophily may create echo chambers for health behavior norms that amplify the divergence we document.

Our findings suggest that the familiar sociological insight — that social identities structure behavior through mechanisms beyond individual preference — applies forcefully to partisan identity in the health domain.
The implication is not simply that partisans disagreed about COVID-19, but that the same dynamics of group conformity, symbolic boundary-making, and habitus that sociologists have long studied in the contexts of race, religion, and class now operate through partisanship as well.
Understanding how to bridge these divides during health crises — or, more ambitiously, how to prevent health from becoming partisan terrain in the first place — requires not just better information or clearer messaging, but a sociological understanding of why people adopt the health behaviors their group expects of them, even when those behaviors may not serve their material interests.
